planting.note: Plant container-grown or field-dug trees in spring in any well-drained to moist, slightly acidic to alkaline, sandy to loamy soil in full sun to partial shade; cabbage palm transplants remarkably well even as a large tree and quickly re-establishes its root system. Remove all fronds at transplanting to reduce transpiration stress.

directions: Plant in any well-drained to moist soil in full sun to partial shade — cabbage palm, the state tree of Florida and South Carolina, is the most cold-hardy of all the large, single-trunk native palms and the defining tree of the southeastern coastal plain from the Carolinas through Florida and around the Gulf Coast. The arching, blue-green fronds make it instantly recognizable as the symbol of the South. It is remarkably adaptable — tolerating salt spray, flooding, drought, alkaline soil, and hurricane-force winds (the flexible trunk bends dramatically but rarely breaks). White flower clusters in summer attract pollinators; the small black fruits feed dozens of bird species and are an important food source for cedar waxwings, fish crows, and raccoons. The growing bud ('heart of palm' or 'swamp cabbage') is edible but harvesting kills the tree. It transplants unusually well even when large, as shown by the millions of palms transplanted annually in Florida landscaping.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in spring in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; saw palmetto transplants from containers fairly well but is very slow to establish from small plants. The underground root system is ancient and extensive in wild specimens.

directions: Plant in sandy, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade — saw palmetto is one of the most ecologically important shrubs in the southeastern US, forming the dominant understory layer across millions of acres of Florida and Gulf Coast pine flatwoods and scrub. Individual plants are estimated to live 500–700 years, maintaining an enormous root system that stores carbohydrates and re-sprouts after fire within days. The cream-white flower clusters produce small black berries that are a primary food source for black bears, white-tailed deer, gopher tortoises, and more than 100 bird species. The berries are also harvested commercially as an herbal supplement for prostate health. Saw palmetto tolerates partial shade, salt, drought, and periodic flooding — it is one of the most resilient and valuable native plants for Gulf Coast habitat gardens. The saw-toothed petioles give it its name.

planting.note: Plant container-grown trees in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial shade; bigleaf magnolia requires protection from strong winds, which shred the enormous leaves. Partial afternoon shade is important in zones 7–8.

directions: Plant in moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil sheltered from strong winds in partial shade; bigleaf magnolia holds the distinction of producing the largest simple leaves of any native tree in North America — up to 32 inches long and 12 inches wide — and the combination of these extraordinary leaves with the enormous, intensely fragrant white flowers (up to 16 inches across) makes this one of the most spectacular ornamental trees in the world. The cream-white flowers in late spring to early summer have a distinctive sweet fragrance detectable from considerable distance. It grows naturally as a rare understory tree in moist, protected ravines and hollows of the South Appalachians and Gulf States. Site it in a protected, partly shaded location away from wind — the huge leaves shred easily in exposed sites. The rose-pink fruiting cones in fall are ornamental and provide food for birds.

planting.note: Surface sow seed in early spring or fall in dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral, well-drained to poor soil in full sun to partial shade; sweet goldenrod self-sows and naturalizes readily in well-drained areas and is not aggressive in ordinary garden conditions.

directions: Sow seed on the surface of dry to moderately moist, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade in early spring; sweet goldenrod is unique among goldenrods for its exceptionally strong anise-licorice fragrance in the leaves and stems — when the foliage is crushed or brushed, the pure, clean anise scent is released. This fragrance comes from trans-anethole, the same compound that flavors anise seed, licorice, and tarragon. Historically, sweet goldenrod was used as an anise tea substitute known as 'Blue Mountain Tea,' and the leaves are still used to make an herbal tea with a pleasant anise flavor. Like all goldenrods, the flowers are an exceptional late-season nectar source for monarchs, native bees, and butterflies. It tolerates partial shade and dry, poor soils — performing in conditions where many goldenrods fail. The only goldenrod with significant culinary and herbal value.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 2–3 inches deep in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun in fall; meadow garlic naturalizes readily and spreads by bulblets and bulbils — allow it to colonize in a meadow or woodland edge setting where spreading is welcome.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; meadow garlic is a widespread native Allium found in moist meadows, woodland edges, and disturbed areas throughout eastern North America — an edible wildflower with a mild garlic-onion flavor. The entire plant is edible: bulbs (used like small garlic cloves), leaves (used like chives), and the bulbils (tiny bulblets produced in the flower head instead of or alongside seeds). It is one of the most cold-hardy and adaptable native edible bulbs and tolerates partial shade in moist conditions. In colonial times it was used as a spring potherb, and it remains a foraging favorite. The plant self-sows and spreads vegetatively by bulblets to naturalize in appropriate conditions. Plants die back in summer heat; mark the location of dormant bulbs to avoid digging them inadvertently.
